specialists tend to zoom in well, can drill down to the core details of the problem
generalists tend to zoom out well, can borrow solutions from other domains

but the expert recognizes that Knowledge is a fractal, and has both tools in their kit

you know how on a cold dark night youre sitting indoors trying to take a picture of the stars outside 

but no matter how you try you always get a reflection in the window of the lights already on in your house and it outshines the stars. it kinda acts as a one way mirror

it’s similar trying to accurately model outside your mind (eg reality, or someone else’s mind), where you might have a general idea of whats in the picture but your own thoughts/frameworks/biases act as a blind spot over specific crucial details you’re trying to observe

the more self-aware you are, the better you know which lights (biases) are on and where in your house. and you can try to “correct” for it

but even better: the clearer your mind is, the dimmer your internal lights are, and the better you can see what truly is out there

smth i’ve been thinking abt recently: 

1. optimality is always subject to environment constraints (whether visible or not)

2. striving for optimality under a goldilocks zone of constraints (not too strict/lenient) is where youre forced to bump into creativity

a specific example for #1 is peoples’ thoughts/frameworks/worldviews — it’s easy to dismiss others perspectives as flawed but they arrived at their own default “optimal* state” given their unique set of constraints**

*: “optimal” here is not a value judgment. if i’m raised in an environment where i don’t have to look after anyone else, that won’t be a constraint for me and so my “optimal” state would be, objectively, selfish
**: it really has to do more with what they PERCEIVE to be their set of constraints. this may not line up for many reasons but a big one imo is that constraints are dynamic

i’ve been trying to figure out how to phrase this in a non-right-wing way but inclusion comes at a tradeoff

obv inclusion is a great ideal in a vacuum, and i’ll take for granted that i don’t need to explain why

but adding someone into an idea bubble results in a “merge conflict”

you can either
1. dilute the idea to meet the new person in their context
2. assimilate the person into the idea bubble by adding context

in other words, either the idea or the person (or some combo) has to be reframed to resolve this merge conflict
if you’re completely changing the idea to meet where your audience is at, it won’t be interesting to them at all — it’s old hat
otoh if you’re assimilating someone into a new context, they’ll have to put in the work to “fit in” (ie restructure their thinking)

this is the major issue i have with current media literacy — the point of art is to try and see things the way the artist sees it. it requires you, the viewer, to reframe your mind! some critics argue instead for clarity, bc things could be misinterpreted. but this is dilution!!

you can liken this to a false subject-object duality

“i want to observe the object without changing me (the subject) or it (the object)”

unfortunately, the “object” does not exist without a “subject”! it’s only a placeholder, re-animated differently each time by each “subject”
ultimately there’s a sweet-spot, a tightrope walk, a balancing of tensions that artists do to manage this tradeoff of quality/quantity of audience resonance

@animalologist kind of in tandem with that, the platforms also reduce the action space within which we interact, which necessarily makes indicators less reliable

@animalologist e.g. if you're a teacher and you want to reliably assess how well your students understand concepts, free response will be more informative than multiple choice. there's a larger action space, so it's easier to disambiguate intent

@animalologist whereas for MC you don't know if your student just happened to guess right. it's harder to map action back to intent in a smaller action space bc the actions get overloaded with possible meanings. it's less of a reliable indicator
@animalologist but if you're grading 100 students, you don't have time to read through 100 free response essays! you'll need to make some sort of tradeoff between reliability and scale

@animalologist a hiring manager may mistakenly optimize for reach in advertising an open role. but there's limited resources ppl on the team can spend on interviewing. they'll filter out most candidates early on, based on a smaller action space (and one that is also easier-to-fake): a resume

codification is a feedback loop

the more problem X becomes codified with solution Y, the more social pressure / confusion you face trying to solve X without Y

eventually the association gets so strong that ppl ask how we solved X before Y existed (or assume we didn’t)

—> focus on what you want to see more of https://t.co/rm4xHw39YF
this “fabrication” is not obvious when you’ve taken on the identity. it seems reasonable, automatic even! it’s a lens through which you’re tricked into seeing more of what you *don’t* want

another related-ish example:
couple months ago, my friend was talking abt a coworker (6’+, buff) who he’d started going to the gym with. one day my friend mentioned he was too busy to go, and the guy threatened: “don’t ever cancel on me the day of again. that’s disrespectful.”

last week i was having fun at the bar with this friend. after a while he mentioned he had agreed to a plan later that night with coworkers (buff dude included) and had to go, but just didn’t want to. “whatever, i’ll just text him next morning and tell him i was sick”

and smth clicked: 
buff dude hates when ppl cancel on him 
—> ppl are scared to cancel on him 
—> he gets ghosted, bc they feel they have no alternative
—> he cracks down against ppl canceling on him, to end the problem once and for all

…rinse and repeat

entrenching your identity in reacting against smth only solidifies its existence. it might seem inexplicable but it’s a feedback loop. internally, you view everything through that lens. externally, you create an environment geared towards the action you’re trying to stop

and there’s so many societal dichotomies that are falling down this path. i’m starting to see this same feedback loop in other places: men v women, why two of my friends are in a (lowkey toxic) situationship lol, democrats v republicans (though im more entrenched here)

what’s the answer? focus on what you want to see *more* of! this is what breaks the invisible feedback loop, bc now your identity doesn’t require an “other”

it’s not the easy path, or the vindicating path, but long-term it’s the more fruitful path 🍊
